mnemonics.title: Master Theorem Cases
mnemonics.content: If f(n) is poly-smaller: Case 1 (n^logb a). If f(n) equals: Case 2 (n^logb a log n). If f(n) is poly-larger with regularity: Case 3 (f(n)).

mnemonics.title: Recurrence Solution Steps
mnemonics.content: HOMOG: Find char eq → Solve roots → Form general solution → Apply initial conditions. NON-HOMOG: Find homogeneous solution + Find particular solution → Combine.

mnemonics.title: Fibonacci Roots
mnemonics.content: Golden ratio φ = (1+√5)/2 and its conjugate - both positive, sum to 1, product is -1/4

acronyms.usage: Used when describing sequences that depend on previous terms
The zero part (right side = 0) of a recurrence relation
The solution obtained from characteristic equation roots
A specific solution to non-homogeneous recurrence

visualTips.visualization: Think of r as the 'growth factor' - if r > 1, sequence grows exponentially; if r = 1, it grows linearly; if |r| < 1, it decays.
Draw three boxes: Case 1 (f(n) smaller), Case 2 (equal/same height), Case 3 (f(n) bigger). The recursive part n^logb(a) is the baseline.
Homogeneous = left side only (all terms with a_n); Non-homogeneous = has constant or function on right side (like +5 or +n²).

analogies.analogy: Like a domino effect - each domino (term) falls and knocks the next one, with the rule of how far each pushes being the recurrence.
Finding the 'DNA' or 'code' of the recurrence - the roots tell us the fundamental pattern of how the sequence grows.
It's like comparing three runners: the recursive work (aT(n/b)) vs the combining work (f(n)). Who wins determines the final time complexity.
Finding one specific path through a maze - it's one valid way through, while the homogeneous solution represents all possible general patterns.
